results.title: Frontier AI fails 97.5% of remote tasks, study shows. | Rex Liu posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content:
Frontier AI just failed >97.5% of real-world remote tasks. The new Remote Labor Index (Center for AI Safety, Dan Hendrycks) tested frontier agents on end-to-end freelance projects like web dev & data analysis. Anthropic's Claude performed best w/ 2.1%, OpenAI's gpt-5 at 1.7%, and Google's gemini at 0.8%. The scaling laws that dominated narratives in the past years aren't delivering on real work. As Andrej Karpathy put it on Dwarkesh's podcast: scaling with RL is fundamentally broken. When a model stumbles upon a correct answer, every token gets upweighted, including reasoning errors. It's noisy/high-variance, and doesn't resemble how humans actually learn. But enterprises aren't waiting for perfect systems. 79% have already deployed AI agents (PwC). If agents stay brittle and jagged for [...] 1

Not because AGI is around the corner, but because it's not. Curious what failure modes you're seeing in your AI systems.

results.title: Watch a Frontier AI Agent Fail at Basic Data Science
results.content:
This is the problem I am hearing from the Analytics teams I work with. They are interested in using AI for data science practices, but too often the data analysis is wrong. When it’s right, it’s like magic, but too often it’s frustrating because it gets it wrong.

I’ve personally been using Claude for survey analysis, and it works really well. But once you get into more sophisticated data sets, the tech just doesn’t hold up.

### Your AI agent is navigating your data with fixed-course steering [...] The video explains that most AI agents today work like Admiral Shovell. They write code, execute it, and assume the result is right. Maybe they check once or twice. Then they move on. But your data has outliers. Missing values. Poorly documented schemas. Things that just don’t make sense until you actually look. If your AI isn’t constantly checking its position, it can be more than half a degree off. And in data science, half a degree off means your decisions are completely wrong.

### You can’t orchestrate what you can’t trust [...] # AI for Insights Leaders

# Watch a Frontier AI Agent Fail at Basic Data Science

### What solution you can use to super charge your data science team that is superior to a frontier model

Kristi Zuhlke

Apr 10, 2026

I met Sphinx AI about 6 months ago, and I’ve been really impressed. If you lead an analytics or data science team, this article is for you. Watch this 20-second clip of Rohan, the Founder of Sphinx, explaining the problem he is solving for data science and analytics.

A frontier coding agent was asked to build a basic linear regression. This is something a junior data scientist can do. And the trend lines it came up with were completely wrong. Skewed by a couple of outliers and minor data issues that any human analyst would have caught.

results.title: The disconnect for AI is that it is a jagged frontier and it only really shines ... | Hacker News
results.content: Curiosity is a precursor to learning. Learning engages the brain and is fun. But it’s not fun all the time, some of it is challenging and frustrating (which is good, that’s the process that teaches you). And I love how I can go from a curious brainfart "hmm, could I do a movie catalogue app that uses a web page + phone camera + OpenAI API to identify physical DVDs by front/back cover instead of trying to find a reliable barcode database" to it actually working in maybe two hours of real time. Just paused the movie I was watching, typed the idea to Claude Code on mobile and kept watching.  After the movie went back to my computer, merged the changes and tested whether it worked. It mostly did. The UI/UX was horrible etc, but the basic idea was functional. It even got some of the movie [...] As I'm reaching a more mature stage of the project though, I'm finding that I want to tweak a lot by hand in the code to get the details right without burning tokens. | | | |  |  | throwaway219450 55 days ago  | parent | next (javascript:void(0))   The agents always do the best work IMO if you already know exactly what you want, but are too lazy to implement it. I like having the agent mock up a working solution before reimplementing it. To split the difference, I now try to hand code as much as I can from the beginning, leave TODO comments for the agent to mop up and I'll ask it to complete the issue with reference to the current diff. It reduces the surface for agents to make stupid assumptions. If I can get it done fast on my own, win for me, if the agent finds issues or there's logic [...] been so much extra crap associated with it (especially web development, and especially corporate web development, what currently pays my bills) over the years that it's worn me down and I'm happy to let A.I. churn through the hard or frustrating or endless amounts of boilerplate bits, and let me focus on other things. Part of me still wishes we were making websites with just HTML, CSS, PHP, and a little Javascript here and there (before AJAX). I'm still not convinced all this extra SPA functionality is really needed for most corporate website needs (something like Google maps or real-time chatting, sure, other things not so much), but I do it because they insist.  I also really like game design, and I had a fairly simple game idea that I prototyped a physical version of and playtested a

results.title: Why Enterprise AI Agents are Currently Broken (And How to Fix It)
results.content: So, if you are running autonomous AI agents, you might be not might be for sure be sitting on massive security and financial time bomb without even realizing it. You know, lately the developer community has been obsessed with MCP as we all know. It's a universal standard that lets you plug any AI model directly into your internal databases, your local files, and your APIs. But while everyone is having fun tinkering with these agents on their laptops, scaling them into the real team introduces two massive production challenges. The first one being uh quite literally devastating security risks, and the second one being the um runaway API bills. So, today we are looking at why the current uh chaotic approach to running agents is fundamentally broken, and how an open source platform called [...] broken, and how an open source platform called orchestra.ai acts as a secure, cost-saving control plane to solve these challenges. I'll leave the links in the description below for you to check it out. So, first of all, let's look at the security nightmare. So, to to let an agent actually do the work, developers often uh run these systems locally on their machines, and they give full system permissions. So, the danger of this setup becomes painfully clear uh during like a massive security exploit involving cloud bot, for example. Right? Uh which is a highly popular uh local assistant uh framework that was later rebranded as Malt book. Very very popular in the news. It It went viral, you know, because it made running these terminal commands via your AI incredibly easy. It was like viral on [...] exponentially pricier because you are constantly repaying for the text the AI already processed. So, it's exponential. It's not like linear. Now, from a cost and ROI perspective, Orchestra can reduce your AI API bills by up to 96%. 96. It achieves this through two main features. First, it uses a dynamic model optimizer. So, instead of sending every simple subtask through expensive frontier grade reasoning models, Orchestra automatically routes basic operations, let's say if you're doing text formatting, extraction, or you know, classification, to cheaper, highly effective models. So, it reserves the expensive models strictly for high-level reasoning and planning. And you instantly cut your baseline API spend. Second, it runs automatic token compression on your tool inputs and outputs. So,

results.title: Becoming a Frontier Firm: A guide for deploying AI agents based on our experience at Microsoft - Inside Track Blog
results.content:
By capitalizing on the lessons we’ve learned during our internal deployment, you can both speed up the process of building and deploying agents at your company while avoiding frustrating pitfalls. If you anchor your work in careful planning and use the steps and resources we’ve provided here, you’ll be on the path toward true business transformation through agentic workflows.

“Embracing AI transformation is an opportunity for IT leaders to take part in defining the future of their organizations. Our role as technical professionals has never been more revolutionary, and our team can support yours as you reimagine workflows to make AI part of your everyday reality.”

Vijaya Alaparthi, principal group product manager, Microsoft Digital [...] Mykhailo Sydorchuk, Customer Zero lead for Microsoft 365 integrated experiences, Microsoft Digital

AI itself is becoming a cornerstone solution for this challenge. An AI-driven approach aligns with the idea of the Frontier Firm, where humans lead and agents operate, in this case by supporting other humans as they explore AI more deeply.

This is a relatively new approach, but we’re already using agents to provide support in several ways:
